source.title: Temporal memory is shaped by encoding stability and intervening item reactivation

confidence_rationale: Direct fMRI evidence that hippocampal pattern stability across items predicts subsequent temporal-order memory; category shifts (event boundaries) interrupt this stability. Validates the within-segment / across-segment distinction MECH-288 architecturally enforces.
failure_signatures: Hippocampal pattern similarity across intervening items predicted serial memory. A substrate where event-segment transitions did NOT update hippocampal anchor state would lose the within/across-segment distinction biology uses for temporal binding.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Hippocampal pattern similarity across intervening items predicts subsequent successful order memory; category shifts (event boundaries) reduce pattern similarity and disrupt order memory.
mapping.ree_translation: Within-segment items share a hippocampal context representation; segment boundaries flush this context. The substrate event_segmenter, by emitting a new segment_id at boundaries, gives downstream consumers (anchor sets, temporal-order representations) the signal needed to implement this within/across distinction.
mapping.mapping_caveat: DuBrow used categorical shifts (face/object) as event-boundary proxies. Whether substrate-detected boundaries from latent change-points produce the same kind of pattern-similarity drop is an empirical question; the architecture supports the right shape of effect.
mapping.source_context: fMRI of healthy human adults; sequence learning of celebrity face / common object stimuli; recency-discrimination test.

confidence_components.notes: JNeurosci paper, well-controlled. Mapping is solid for the architectural claim that segment boundaries should produce hippocampal context discontinuity.
